rationale: The cited paper appears directly relevant as a likely mechanism/theory source: it distinguishes conceptual vs perceptual priming/fluency in advertising and links them to brand-choice outcomes, which closely overlaps the citing paper’s focal constructs (conceptual/perceptual fluency, brand responses). The citing note explicitly positions its contribution against prior consumer work that emphasized conceptual fluency effects on consideration sets/choice rather than evaluation, matching the cited paper’s scope. Given this conceptual continuity and exact DOI match, this is not a clear incidental mention.
